insights: The dataset contains no 'Cost Optimization' goals—neither in category values nor in short_description text—making the original investigation goal fundamentally unanswerable with this data.

All 500 records show 100% success rate (250 'Closed' + 250 'Resolved'), with zero variation across priority, category, location, or assignee—eliminating success rate as a meaningful differentiator for goal management efficiency.

52.8% of records (264/500) have invalid resolution times (closed_at < opened_at), including cases where closed_at falls in 2023 and opened_at in 2024—indicating systemic temporal corruption that invalidates all time-based efficiency metrics.

Negative resolution times are uniformly distributed across all categories, priorities, locations, and assignees—confirming the data quality issue is structural, not segment-specific, and cannot be mitigated by filtering or stratification.

The uniform placeholder text 'There was an issue' across all 500 rows confirms the dataset is synthetic and lacks real-world operational semantics—undermining its utility for diagnosing actual goal management bottlenecks or improvement opportunities.
summary: The dataset cannot support the stated goal of investigating unexpectedly high success rates for Low/Medium-priority 'Cost Optimization' goals because (1) no 'Cost Optimization' goals exist in any field, (2) success rate is uniformly 100% across all dimensions with no variation to analyze, and (3) over half the records contain chronologically impossible timestamps that invalidate time-based efficiency evaluation. These are not analytical limitations but foundational data deficiencies: the absence of target goals, total outcome homogeneity, and pervasive temporal corruption collectively render the dataset unsuitable for deriving actionable insights on goal management efficiency. Next steps must focus on sourcing a production-grade dataset with valid temporal integrity, diverse success outcomes, and accurate goal categorization—including explicit 'Cost Optimization' labeling—before any diagnostic or optimization analysis can proceed.
